q: Electrical energy can be transformed into a wide variety of other useful forms of energy.
The transformation of electrical energy into heat energy is used in many home appliances.
Electrical energy is provided by sources of electromotive force such as batteries and generators.
A steady current will flow through a conductor if a steady potential difference is maintained between its ends.
An electric generator converts chemical energy into electrical energy to provide electrical power.
A battery converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.
Within a source of electromotive force, positive charges are sent from the negative terminal to the positive terminal.
The main function of an electromotive source is to send negative charges from the positive terminal to the negative terminal within the source.
Electromotive force ($emf$) is defined as the amount of energy provided by the battery per unit charge to move round the complete circuit.
Electromotive force is measured in units of volt ($V$), which is equivalent to joule per coulomb ($J\ C^{-1}$).
Despite its name, electromotive force is actually a physical force.
A source of electromotive force generally does not have any internal resistance.
The internal resistance of a battery depends on its size, chemical properties, life time, and temperature.
The potential difference across the terminals of a battery connected to an external circuit is called the terminal voltage.
Terminal voltage is also referred to as available voltage.
The potential difference between the terminals of a battery is always greater than its $emf$ when used in an electric circuit.
Electromotive force ($emf$) is an open-circuit voltage.
Terminal voltage is a closed-circuit voltage.
Charging a battery means the chemical energy of the battery which has been used up is now supplied back by electrical energy from some external source.
In charging a battery, the potential difference between the terminals is equal to $E + Ir$.
The potential difference between the terminals of a battery in an open circuit is zero.
The total work done in moving a unit positive charge round the complete circuit is $IR + Ir$, which is the $emf$ $E$ of the battery.
Potential difference is the amount of energy required to move a unit charge from one end to another end of a circuit element.
When a battery is discharging, the terminal voltage is $V = E - Ir$.
The circuit equation is given by $I = \frac{E}{R+r}$.
When two or more electromotive sources are connected in series, the resultant $emf$ is the algebraic sum of the individual emfs.
In a series aiding connection, the currents leaving the batteries are in the same direction.
The resultant $emf$ for batteries in series aiding is $(E_1 + E_2)$.
The total internal resistance of two batteries in series aiding is $(r_1 + r_2)$.
In a series opposing connection, the emfs of the batteries are in opposition.
The resultant $emf$ of batteries in series opposing is the difference between the individual emfs.
In a series opposing connection, the total internal resistance is $(r_1 - r_2)$.
For two batteries having equal emfs and equal internal resistances connected in parallel, the resultant $emf$ of the parallel-combination is just the $emf$ $E$ of a single battery.
In a parallel connection of batteries, the same polarity terminals of the batteries are connected together.
The resultant internal resistance of two identical batteries in parallel is $\frac{r}{2}$.
In a parallel connection of batteries, care must be taken that the terminals of the same polarity are connected together to avoid damaging the batteries.
The current flowing through each identical battery in a parallel combination is $\frac{I}{2}$.
If $E_1$ is greater than $E_2$ in series opposing, the resultant $emf$ is $(E_1 - E_2)$.
Electrical energy is energy associated with the flow of charge through any part of a circuit.
Electrical energy can be generated from photovoltaic cells.
Electrical energy can be stored for future application using fuel cells and capacitors.
The work done $W$ in bringing the amount of charge $Q$ from point A to B is $W = QV$.
Potential difference is defined as the work done in bringing a unit positive charge from one point to another.
Electrical energy supplied by a battery is transformed into heat energy because electrons collide with the atoms in the resistor.
When electrons collide with atoms in a resistor, the atoms acquire additional energy and produce heat energy.
The electrical energy $W$ produced by a battery is given by $W = VIt = I^2 Rt = \frac{V^2}{R}t$.
An electric motor transforms electrical energy into heat energy instead of mechanical energy.
The practical unit of electrical energy is the kilowatt hour ($kWh$).
One kilowatt hour is equal to $3.60 \times 10^6\ J$.
Electrical power is the rate of transfer of electrical energy.
Electrical power $P$ is expressed as $P = \frac{W}{t}$.
The formulas for electrical power are $P = VI = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$.
The SI unit of electrical power $P$ is the watt ($W$).
One watt is equivalent to $1\ joule\ per\ second$ ($J\ s^{-1}$).
One unit of electricity is defined as $1\ kWh$.
Electrical energy cannot be stored in inductors.
If an electric lamp is connected in a circuit, it converts electrical energy into heat and light energy.
The relation $1\ kWh = 1000\ W \times 1\ h$ is used to convert $kWh$ to joules.
Work $W$ by a battery taking charge $Q$ from A to B is equivalent to the electrical energy supplied by the battery.
Electrical energy can be generated from dynamos and generators.
When electric current passes through a conductor, the electrons are accelerated due to the electric field.
Electrons lose kinetic energy which appears as heat energy when they collide with other atoms in a conductor.
James Prescott Joule experimentally studied the heating effect of electric current in 1841.
The electrical energy $W$ supplied by a battery is related to heat $H$ by the equation $W = JH$.
The constant $J$ is called Joule's mechanical equivalent of heat and its value is $4.2\ J\ cal^{-1}$.
Joule's law of electricity and heat states that heat produced in a resistor is directly proportional to the square of the current.
According to Joule's law, the heat produced is inversely proportional to the resistance of the conductor.
Heat produced in a resistor is directly proportional to the time taken by the current to pass through it.
Joule's law of electricity and heat can be represented by the equation $H = \frac{I^2 Rt}{J}$.
The amount of heat $H$ can also be written as $H = \frac{VIt}{J} = \frac{V^2 t}{RJ}$.
Electric stoves, electric cookers, and electric irons are applications of the heating effect of current.
An electric light bulb consists of a tungsten filament which has a low melting point.
The melting point of a tungsten filament is approximately $3000\ ^{\circ}C$.
In an electric light bulb, air is removed and a small amount of argon, an inert gas, is added.
A tungsten filament emits white light at a temperature of $1800\ ^{\circ}C$.
Adding an inert gas to a light bulb increases the evaporation of the tungsten filament.
A simple light bulb used in a torch has an electrical power of a few watts.
Bulbs used for illuminating airfields have electrical power of the order of $5000\ W$.
An electric iron uses a mica sheet wound around nichrome wire.
Mica is a good electrical insulator and can withstand very high temperatures.
The heating element in an electric iron is sandwiched between two mica sheets and placed on a heavy metal plate.
A thermostat in an electric iron switches the current on and off to maintain a selected temperature.
An electric iron typically has an electrical power of $750-1000\ W$.
In an electric stove, a coil of nichrome wire is placed in a groove of heat-resistant material.
The electrical power of an electric stove ranges from $600\ W$ to $2000\ W$.
According to Table 10.1, a laptop computer uses $15-45\ W$ of electrical power.
A refrigerator uses approximately $100-250\ W$ of electrical power.
A reading lamp has a power rating of $40-100\ W$.
Radio and television receivers use approximately $150\ W$ of electrical power.
A fuse is a thick long wire of tin-lead alloy used to increase current in a circuit.
A fuse is used in electric circuits to prevent electrical appliances from being damaged if excessive current flows through them.
When a current greater than the fuse rating flows in the circuit, the fuse becomes hot enough to melt and break the circuit.
A $3\ A$ fuse is used for appliances rated as $700\ W$ or less in a $220\ V$ circuit.
For appliances above $700\ W$, a $13\ A$ fuse should be fitted.
A fuse wire can be fitted in a glass tube (cartridge) or on a porcelain block.
Nichrome wire produces heat energy when current flows through it in an electric iron.
Domestic electrical appliances usually specify both voltage and power, such as $220\ V$, $60\ W$.
